critique.summary: The article details the author's project to build a reader for the Toshiba MK4001MTD, the world's smallest hard drive at 0.85 inches, originally released in 2004. It covers the history of the drive, challenges in interfacing with it, reverse engineering efforts using a Nokia N91 phone, and the eventual creation of custom hardware and firmware to access the drive via SDIO and ATA commands.
mainPoints: Toshiba introduced the 0.85-inch MK4001MTD hard drive in 2004, recognized as the world's smallest hard disk drive by Guinness World Records.
The drive was used in devices like the Nokia N91, providing 4 GB of storage in a feature phone.
The author attempted to read the drive using standard MMC/SD card readers but found the drive used a non-standard SDIO interface carrying ATA commands.
By reverse engineering signal traces from a Nokia N91 and using a Raspberry Pi Pico with custom firmware, the author successfully accessed the drive as USB mass storage.
The project includes custom hardware design with an RP2040 microcontroller and spring-loaded contacts to interface directly with the drive.
All project files, including firmware and hardware designs, are publicly available on GitHub.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are well-supported by references to official Toshiba press releases, the Nokia N91 user guide, MMCplus documentation, and Guinness World Records. The technical details about the drive's interface and the reverse engineering process align with known facts about the MK4001MTD and its use in the Nokia N91. The project appears to be a genuine, detailed engineering effort with verifiable sources and plausible technical explanations.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential risks of data loss during the reverse engineering and testing process, which the author notes did occur occasionally.
There is limited discussion on the practical applications or demand for accessing such obsolete hardware today.
The performance limitations of the custom reader (e.g., slow data rates) and how they compare to modern storage solutions could be further explored.
Potential challenges in replicating the project for users without advanced electronics skills or equipment are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the impressive reverse engineering of a small hard drive, with some commenters reminiscing about old technology and others questioning certain details or expressing confusion. There is appreciation for the technical effort, alongside some light humor and requests for additional media.

comments.author: mkirsten
comments.text:
Still a great story, great page and impressive reverse engineering. Isn't OpenClaw vs. he did it "himself" a bit analogue to the critique "oh, he wrote the firmware in C++ and let the compiler figure out the assembly code".

Maybe you wanted to see the assembly code, and that's fine. But he took a potentially difficult problem, found tools to solve it and documented (to some degree) the process?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on the reverse engineering approach, emphasizing the value of using tools and documenting the process rather than focusing solely on low-level coding.

comments.author: alias_neo
comments.text:
I remember my Nokia N91 which had a 4GB version of one of these tiny HDDs, blew my mind at the time.

Man do I miss the N-series, I had so many good phones in that era.
comments.analysis: This nostalgic comment adds context about the historical use of small HDDs in consumer electronics, which is consistent with known technology history.

comments.author: Tempest1981
comments.text:
Cool!

I found the old drive that worked with my Canon camera. It's a Hitachi 2GB Microdrive from 2003. It says CF+ Type-II. So larger, with a CompactFlash interface, boring in comparison.

More history: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microdrive

I'm trying to remember the camera... Canon Powershot S1 IS maybe? It used a lot more battery running the microdrive.
comments.analysis: This comment provides relevant historical information about microdrives and their use in cameras, supporting the discussion with a credible personal anecdote.

comments.author: thehamkercat
comments.text:
"Coded by OpenClaw" written on PCB

was this necessary? could've said "Code written by LLM" or something
comments.analysis: This comment uses humor and sarcasm to question the labeling on the PCB, but it does not add substantive technical insight.

comments.author: tda
comments.text: Is there a video somewhere of the one inch microdrive with acrylic display shown in the article?
comments.analysis: A straightforward request for additional media that could enhance understanding, showing engagement but no technical analysis.

comments.author: wartywhoa23
comments.text: Lost me at the first OpenClaw mention.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses confusion without contributing to the discussion or providing further insight.
